heading: libro della Guida69. Perciò gli ebrei più sapienti ammettono spontaneamente che quell'oracolo si riferisce al Messia, come si legge in una
card_summary: This chunk belongs to the Seventh Tone (Tono Settimo) of the Secondo Cantico and opens by establishing the messianic identity of the "Lord" in Psalm 110 ("Siedi alla mia destra"), citing Paul's letters to the Ephesians and Hebrews as exegetical keys. Zorzi then works through three successive chapters explaining why Christ's eternal kingdom was promised specifically to the house of Jacob rather than to Abraham or Isaac: Abraham figures the universal Father whose household contains both legitimate and illegitimate offspring (Isaac/Ishmael as Christ and the Devil born from "spurious" creative delight); Isaac figures the twofold generation of animal and spiritual man (Esau/Jacob), with Christ unable to reign where carnal rebellion persists. The chapter on Jacob (Ch. 5) develops an elaborate typological parallel between Jacob and Christ as patriarchs of a twelve-membered elect family, then allegorises Jacob's four women as the active and contemplative lives (Leah and Rachel) paired with their handmaids (the lower rational faculty and sense-perception). A lengthy digression on the number seven — drawing on medical, biblical, and Neoplatonic sources — explains why Jacob's double seven-year servitude encodes the soul's double purification before it reaches contemplation. Sources cited include Maimonides (Guida), rabbinic glosses on Genesis, Paul (Ephesians, Hebrews, 1 Corinthians), John (Apocalypse), Malachi, Proverbs, Job, Leviticus, Plotinus, and Jerome.
essay:
## Overview

This summary covers the opening portion of chunk 82 (approximately the first 600 lines of a 53,504-word section), corresponding to the beginning of the Seventh Tone (Tono Settimo) of the Secondo Cantico in Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi*. The section is structured as a continuous exegetical treatise in numbered chapters (Capp. I–V visible in this portion) asking a single governing question: why does the angelic oracle in Luke 1 declare that Christ "will reign over the house of Jacob forever" rather than over the houses of Abraham or Isaac?

---

## Chapter II: Psalm 110 and the Messianic Lord

Zorzi opens by invoking Maimonides's *Guida dei perplessi* (note 69) as evidence that even the wisest Jewish interpreters admit that the oracle "Il Signore disse al mio Signore: Siedi alla mia destra" (Ps 110:1) refers to the Messiah. The argument is that David, speaking under the Holy Spirit, could not have called any of his own subjects or any foreign king "my Lord" — the referent must be the Messiah, who transcends the entire political order.

Paul's letter to the Ephesians (Eph 1:20–22) provides the explicit christological key in the Latin embedded in the bilingual text:

> Deus Pater suscitavit Christum a mortuis constituens ad dexteram suam in coelestibus supra omnem principatum, potestatem, virtutem, et dominationem, et omne nomen quod nominatur non solum in hoc saeculo, sed etiam in futuro, et omnia subiecit sub pedibus eius.

<cite>p. 1656 (Cantico Secondo, Tono Settimo)</cite>

The letter to the Hebrews (Heb 1:13–14) adds that no angel was ever told to sit at God's right hand; angels are merely "spiriti servienti" — administrative spirits, not lords. Therefore the prophet speaks of one who commands in the heavens and dominates over all earthly kings.

---

## Chapter III: Why Not the House of Abraham?

Zorzi argues that Abraham represents the universal Father of all nations — hence the name-change from Abram to Abraham ("padre di molte e diverse genti," with appeal to Gen 17). Abraham's household contains both legitimate and illegitimate offspring (Isaac from Sarah; Ishmael from Hagar), which makes him an image of the divine Father whose generative activity divides into two ontologically distinct acts.

The first act is the eternal, "legitimate" union of the divine Intellect with its own essence — a self-contemplation from which the only-begotten Son proceeds, fittingly named Isaac (= "ilarità e gioia del padre," citing the heavenly voice of Matt 3:17). The second act is a "spurious" or willed productive delight (*complacens intuitio*) through which God generated the ideas of all things and fashioned external creation — from which, at the apex of that created order, arose the angel who became the Devil.

> Dal primo atto di creazione spuria è nato colui del quale il Signore dice, nel libro di Giobbe: Egli è l'inizio delle vie di Dio. Si tratta, ne convengono tutti i sapienti, del principe dei ribelli, colui che voleva opprimere il Figlio legittimo.

<cite>p. 1659 (Cantico Secondo, Tono Settimo)</cite>

Zorzi is careful to distinguish the devil's created dignity from Christ's eternal generation: Paul's assertion that the Son is "not of this creation" but "generated before all ages" (citing Heb) separates the two modes rigorously. The devil's rebellion against the legitimate heir — narrated via Apoc 12 (the dragon besieging the woman clothed in the sun) — results in the command "Caccia via l'ancella e suo figlio," interpreted as the expulsion both of Lucifer and of the divine delight (*delectatio*) associated with his creation. Zorzi offers a Neoplatonic theodicy: God's delight in creatures does not constitute an external need but flows from the craftsman's recognition of his own image in his work (*imago* / *vestigium*), citing Proverbs 8:31 ("La mia delizia è stare con i figli degli uomini"). This delight-as-handmaid is expelled when the creature rebels, stripping the angel of the Father's image and turning the most beautiful creature into a hideous serpent. Because Abraham's house still harbours this unresolved warfare, Christ the Rex pacificus cannot yet reign there.

---

## Chapter IV: Why Not the House of Isaac?

Isaac in turn figures the divine Artificer who generates man in two dimensions — animal and spiritual — corresponding to Paul's "Prius quod animale, deinde quod spirituale" (1 Cor 15:46) and to Moses's double act of forming the body from dust and then breathing the *spiraculum vitarum* into his nostrils (Gen 2:7). Esau and Jacob are twins from a single womb: Esau (= Edom, "rosso e sanguigno") represents the animal-carnal dimension that God "hates" (Mal 1:2–3); Jacob represents the spiritual.

The spiritual cannot reign where the carnal persists unredeemed. Zorzi marshals Christ's own words to Pilate ("Il mio regno non è di questo mondo," Jn 18:36), the epistle of James ("Chi vuol essere amico di questo mondo, si fa nemico di Dio"), and Paul's "Caro e sanguis regnum Dei possidere non possunt" (1 Cor 15:50). Paul's concluding demand — "Bisogna che questo corpo corruttibile si rivesta di incorruttibilità" — shows that the carnal must be transformed into spirit, not simply suppressed.

The allegory develops further: Esau, if he persists in rebellion, becomes a member of Ishmael's family and a child of the Devil; the animal man who willingly submits to the spiritual receives a secondary share of Isaac's blessing (dew of heaven, abundance of wheat, wine, and oil). Zorzi glosses Isaac's blessing (Gen 27:28–29) in a four-part eucharistic and pneumatological key:

> Il vero frumento è quello che serve a impastare il pane vivo disceso dal cielo. Il vino è pigiato da quella vite che disse: Io sono la vera vite. L'olio, è quello del quale il divino suonatore di cetra canta: Dio ti ha consacrato con olio di letizia.

<cite>p. 1665 (Cantico Secondo, Tono Settimo)</cite>

The phrase "dew of Hermon" is glossed etymologically: Hermon = "anatema della tristezza," so the dew from Hermon means grace poured from a realm where all sorrow is banished. "Secretiores theologi" (esoteric theologians, a recurring Zorziean formula for kabbalistic authorities) are cited to explain that dew corresponds numerically to the lower man's irrigation.

---

## Chapter V: Why the House of Jacob — and the Jacob/Christ Typology

The positive answer to the chapter's governing question occupies the longest section. Jacob and Christ share an extended structural typology: both are patriarchs of a twelve-membered family (Jacob's twelve sons figuring the twelve apostles); both give their own name to their descendants (Israelites / Christians); both are called "prince of God" — Israel means "principe o erede di Dio," while Christ is "prince of the kings of the earth" (Apoc 1:5, attributed to John).

Zorzi pre-empts the obvious objection that Jacob's biography — the deception of Esau, the polygamy, the concubinage — seems morally incompatible with a Christ-figure. His response is programmatic:

> Quicquid enim circa hunc virum gestum est, ad sacramenta altissima pertinet. Supplantavit maiorem natu, et eripuit benedictionem ab eo, qui carnem et sanguinem effigiat, sicut facere debet verus homo spiritualis.

<cite>p. 1671 (Cantico Secondo, Tono Settimo)</cite>

Jacob's journey to Mesopotamia = the soul's vocation to the celestial realms. Jacob's vision of the ladder (Gen 28) = the scala virtutum whose base is planted in the heart (a "valley of tears") and whose apex touches heaven, traversed by ascending and descending angels bearing prayers upward and graces downward. The four women are allegorised as follows:

- **Leah** (laboriosa, weak-eyed) = the **vita activa** (Martha), whose vision is obscured by external multiplicity.
- **Rachel** (beautiful, "she who sees the principle") = the **vita contemplativa** (Mary), whose inner eye gazes on God as the beautiful principle of all things. Zorzi quotes Plotinus directly: "Ognuno diventi bello e simile a Dio, se si appresta a contemplare la bellezza divina."
- The **two handmaids** represent the lower rational-discursive faculty (attached to sensible things) and sense-perception itself.

The requirement that Jacob must first serve for Leah before obtaining Rachel encodes the spiritual law that active charity must precede contemplation — a principle Zorzi grounds in the desert-father tradition (candidates for contemplative life required eighteen to twenty years of active service).

---

## The Number Seven and Double Purification

A long medical-cosmological excursus on the number seven explains Jacob's double seven-year servitude. Drawing on ancient medical doctrine (crises in fevers, stages of infant development from seven hours to seven decades), Zorzi argues that seven is a "numero critico, purgativo e remissivo." The full developmental arc of human life (seventy years, Ps 90:10) is structured by multiples of seven, and the twice-seven years Jacob serves encode the soul's double transformation "di chiarezza in chiarezza" (2 Cor 3:18) necessary before it can bear the contemplative vision. Biblical parallels include Naaman's sevenfold washing in the Jordan (2 Kings 5), the sevenfold sprinkling of the leper (Lev 14), the sabbatical year, the Jubilee (seventh squared = 49), and Christ's answer to Peter that forgiveness extends "seventy times seven" times (Matt 18:22).

---

## Technical Terms and Key Concepts

- *Spurius* / *legitimus* (spurious/legitimate generation): Zorzi's ontological distinction between the eternal procession of the Son and the volitional creation of angels and the world.
- *Complacens intuitio* / *delectatio*: the divine delight in creation, which functions as the "handmaid" expelled with the rebel angel.
- *Syndéresis* and *portio rationalis*: the two faculties of the soul corresponding to Rachel and Leah's handmaids.
- *Ros* (dew): a kabbalistic technical term (cited via "secretiores theologi") corresponding numerically to a specific sefirah or divine influx into the lower man.
- *Scala virtutum*: Jacob's ladder read as an Neoplatonic-Christian ascent structure.
- *Rex pacificus*: Christ's title as the peaceful king who cannot reign in a household still containing active rebellion.

---

## Connections to Zorzi's Larger Project

This section exemplifies Zorzi's characteristic method in the *De Harmonia Mundi*: a question posed in the idiom of Christian biblical exegesis is progressively deepened through Neoplatonic metaphysics (the distinction of legitimate/spurious generation echoes Plotinus on the procession of Intellect), kabbalistic numerology (dew, seven, the quadruple fountain), and patristic allegory (the active/contemplative life from Origen and the desert tradition). The patriarchal narratives become a layered cosmological code in which the structure of the created order — legitimate Son, fallen first-created angel, dual human nature, the soul's itinerary through action to contemplation — is inscribed. The governing thesis that Christ "reigns over the house of Jacob in eternity" is thus not merely a christological proof-text but a statement about the harmonic structure of reality: only where the full hierarchy of spiritual over animal, contemplative over active, Spirit over flesh has been established can the Rex pacificus take up his reign.
